title: Cutting a housing joint
subtitle: Mark the dado, saw the shoulders, chisel out the waste
excerpt: How to cut a through-housing joint across the grain in a shelf or stair stringer: marking the joint width and depth, sawing the shoulder lines, and chiselling out the housing to a flat, even bottom.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (c. 1905). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

recipeTools.notes: A router plane is the best tool for flattening the housing bottom to a consistent depth; a smoothing plane can be used in its absence.

glossaryTerms.definition: A joint in which a shelf or partition member sits in a trench cut across the grain of the supporting panel or wall. Provides strong resistance to vertical load. Called a dado in North American woodworking.
The slot cut by a saw blade. The width of the kerf is the combined thickness of the blade and the set (the degree to which the teeth are bent alternately to each side).

content.text: A
housing joint

provides a much stronger shelf connection than screws alone: the shelf sits in a trench, and the resistance to downward load is born across the full width of the joint, not just at the fastener points. It is the standard joint for fixed shelving in built-in bookcases, alcove shelving, and stairs. The technique described here is the hand-tool method using a tenon saw and chisel.
What you need
Tenon saw.
For cutting the shoulder lines of the housing.
25 mm and 12 mm bevel-edge chisels.
For chopping and paring the waste from the housing.
Marking knife.
For scoring the shoulder lines cleanly across the grain.
Wooden mallet.
For driving the chisel vertically into the housing.
A straight-edged guide.
A second piece of timber clamped to the work to guide the saw in a straight line across the grain.
Step-by-step
Step 1, mark the housing width.

Lay the shelf piece on the face of the board and mark both edges with a pencil. Score both pencil lines with the marking knife and a square. The scored line severs the grain fibres and prevents breakout at the housing edge.
Step 2, mark the housing depth.

Set a marking gauge to one third of the board thickness (for a 18 mm shelf board, this is 6 mm depth). Run the gauge along the edge of the board to mark the depth on both sides. The housing depth should not exceed one third of the board thickness, or the board is significantly weakened.
Step 3, saw the shoulder lines.
Clamp a straight-edge guide along one of the housing lines. Saw down the shoulder line with the tenon saw, keeping the saw on the waste side of the marked line. The saw
kerf
must stay in the waste; if it cuts into the housing it widens the joint and the shelf will be loose. Saw to the depth line marked on both edges. Repeat on the second shoulder.
Step 4, remove the waste.

Working from the face of the board, drive the 25 mm chisel vertically into the housing with the mallet at intervals of about 10 mm across the width, cutting to about half depth. Then work the chisel horizontally from the edge to lever out the chips. Repeat until the housing is cleared to approximately the full depth.
Step 5, pare the bottom flat.

The bottom of the housing should be flat and at a consistent depth along its full length. Work the 25 mm chisel, bevel-up, horizontally along the housing bottom in paring strokes to remove the ridges left by the vertical chops. Check flatness with a steel rule laid across the housing.
What it looks like when it is right

The shelf piece should slide into the housing with firm hand pressure. It should sit level across its full width with the faces of the shelf and the housing board flush. The joint should not be loose enough to rattle, and should not need more than a mallet blow to close.
Common mistakes
Saw on the wrong side of the line.
The housing becomes too wide; the shelf sits loose. Score the line again and use the outer face of the score as the saw guide.
Chiselling past the marked depth.
Weakens the board. Work slowly, checking depth frequently with the marking gauge at the edge of the board.
Uneven housing bottom.
The shelf rocks in the housing. Pare from the centre outward and check flatness at each pass.
